*[[Steven Cheung (political advisor)|Steven Cheung]] *[[Boris Epshteyn]] *[[Karoline Leavitt]] *''...[[#Agenda|and others]]''}} | receipts = {{US$|448,966,052{{Cite web|date=February 6, 2025|title=Donald Trump (R)|url=https://www.opensecrets.org/2024-presidential-race/donald-trump/candidate?id=N00023864|website=[[OpenSecrets]]|access-date=April 12, 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250402213651/https://www.opensecrets.org/2024-presidential-race/donald-trump/candidate?id=N00023864|archive-date=April 2, 2025}}}} | fec_date = | slogan = {{Unbulleted list |[[Make America Great Again]] |[[America First (policy)|America First!]]{{Cite news|last=Minnaugh|first=Ryan|date=April 4, 2025|title=Federal judge finds Trump's 'America First' slogan is racist toward immigrants|url=https://nbcmontana.com/news/nation-world/federal-judge-finds-trumps-america-first-slogan-is-racist-toward-immigrants|access-date=April 12, 2025|work=[[NBC Montana]]|archive-date=April 4, 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250404191113/https://nbcmontana.com/news/nation-world/federal-judge-finds-trumps-america-first-slogan-is-racist-toward-immigrants|url-status=live}} |Never Surrender!{{Cite news|last=Kincade|first=Clayton|date=June 20, 2024|title=6 things Trump says at his rallies and what they really mean|url=https://www.npr.org/2024/06/20/g-s1-4833/trump-too-big-to-rig-drill-baby-swamp-the-vote-rally-maga-2024|access-date=April 12, 2025|work=[[NPR]]|archive-date=October 5, 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241005143332/https://www.npr.org/2024/06/20/g-s1-4833/trump-too-big-to-rig-drill-baby-swamp-the-vote-rally-maga-2024|url-status=live}} |[[Make America Healthy Again]]{{cite news|last1=Kennedy|first1=Robert F|url=https://www.wsj.com/opinion/trump-can-make-america-healthy-again-rfk-jr-reforms-chronic-disease-crisis-a9b4b8c0|title=Trump Can Make America Healthy Again|work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|access-date=October 13, 2024|archive-date=October 8, 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241008064515/https://www.wsj.com/opinion/trump-can-make-america-healthy-again-rfk-jr-reforms-chronic-disease-crisis-a9b4b8c0|url-status=live|url-access=subscription}} |Too Big to Rig |Swamp the Vote |[[Drill, baby, drill]] |[[No Tax on Tips]] |Make America Greater than Ever Before |Trump Will Fix It{{cite news|first=Brett|last=Samuels|date=October 27, 2024|url=https://thehill.com/homenews/campaign/4956332-5-takeaways-trumps-madison-square-garden-rally/|title=5 takeaways from Trump's Madison Square Garden rally|work=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]]|access-date=April 11, 2025|archive-date=April 11, 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411125117/https://thehill.com/homenews/campaign/4956332-5-takeaways-trumps-madison-square-garden-rally/|url-status=live}} }} | themesong = "[[God Bless the U.S.A.]]" by [[Lee Greenwood]]
"[[Hold On, I'm Comin' (song)|Hold On, I'm Comin']]" by [[Sam & Dave]]
"[[Merle Haggard discography#Music videos|America First]]" by [[Merle Haggard]]
"[[Y.M.C.A. (song)|Y.M.C.A.]]" by [[Village People]] | chant = {{Unbulleted list |"USA!" |"Fight! Fight! Fight!" }} | website = [https://web.archive.org/web/20241104222959/https://www.donaldjtrump.com/ www.donaldjtrump.com] (as of November 4, 2024) }} {{US 2024 presidential elections series}} {{Donald Trump series|expanded=Campaigns}} [[Donald Trump]], the [[List of presidents of the United States|45th president of the United States]] (2017–2021) ran a successful campaign for the [[2024 United States presidential election|2024 U.S. presidential election]]. He formally announced his campaign on November 15, 2022, initially battling for the [[2024 Republican Party presidential primaries|Republican Party's nomination]]. While many candidates challenged the former president for the nomination, they did not manage to amass enough support, leading Trump to a [[landslide victory]] in the [[2024 Iowa Republican presidential caucuses|2024 Iowa caucuses]]. On March 12, 2024, he became the [[Republican Party (United States)|Republican Party]]'s [[Presidential nominee|presumptive nominee]]. Trump was officially nominated on July 15 at the [[2024 Republican National Convention|Republican National Convention]], where he chose [[JD Vance]], the junior [[United States Senate|U.S. senator]] from [[Ohio]], as his vice presidential running mate. On November 5, Trump and Vance were elected [[Second presidency of Donald Trump|president]] and [[Vice presidency of JD Vance|vice president]] of the United States, winning all seven [[swing state]]s{{Efn|The swing states in 2024 were [[Wisconsin]], [[Michigan]], [[Pennsylvania]], [[North Carolina]], [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]], [[Arizona]], and [[Nevada]].}} as well as the popular vote with a [[Plurality (voting)|plurality]]. Trump's agenda was branded as [[Right-wing populism|populist]] and [[American nationalism|nationalist]]. It pledged sweeping tax cuts, a [[Protectionism|protectionist]] trade policy, greater federal oversight over education,{{Efn|However, Trump simultaneously promised to shut down the [[Department of Education]].}} more extensive use of [[fossil fuel]]s, an "[[America First (policy)|America First]]" foreign policy, an expansion of presidential authority, a reduction of [[Code of Federal Regulations|federal regulations]], mass deportation of [[Illegal immigration to the United States|illegal immigrants]],{{Efn|While Trump's proposed deportation program primarily targeted illegal immigrants, he also pledged to displace legal immigrants.|name=Deportation}} stricter [[Law enforcement in the United States|law enforcement]], an end to [[diversity, equity, and inclusion]] programs, and a rollback of [[Transgender rights in the United States|transgender rights]]. While the campaign's official platform was [[Agenda 47]], it was closely connected to [[The Heritage Foundation]]'s [[Project 2025]], a playbook recommending an [[Authoritarianism|authoritarian]], rigidly [[Conservatism in the United States|conservative]] state. Trump's [[Rhetoric of Donald Trump|rhetoric]], regarded as inflammatory and extreme and featuring [[disinformation]] and [[fearmongering]], drew immense media coverage. He sought to establish himself as a political [[martyr]] being targeted by the [[The Establishment|political and media establishment]], and that his campaign was one of vindication and a battle between [[good and evil]]. On the campaign trail, Trump faced numerous legal troubles, culminating in [[Indictments against Donald Trump|four indictments]] and a [[Prosecution of Donald Trump in New York|felony conviction]]. Court cases also arose concerning his eligibility to run in the aftermath of the [[January 6 United States Capitol attack|January 6, 2021 Capitol attack]], which were [[Trump v. Anderson|eventually resolved]]. Trump also survived a minor injury in [[Attempted assassination of Donald Trump in Pennsylvania|an assassination attempt]]. Many commentators state that these setbacks helped his [[Public image of Donald Trump|public image]]. The campaign's success was attributed to an effective media strategy, a distinct appeal to younger, male, and minority voters, and a strong focus on the public's political and economic concerns. ==Origins== === Background === Donald Trump's 2024 presidential campaign is his fourth, following a [[Donald Trump 2000 presidential campaign|brief one in 2000]] for the [[Reform Party of the United States of America|Reform Party]]'s nomination, and two as the [[Republican Party (United States)|Republican Party]]'s candidate, in [[Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign|2016]] and, subsequently, [[Donald Trump 2020 presidential campaign|2020]].{{Cite news |last=Helmore |first=Edward |date=February 5, 2017 |title=How Trump's political playbook evolved since he first ran for president in 2000 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/feb/05/donald-trump-reform-party-2000-president |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411112502/https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/feb/05/donald-trump-reform-party-2000-president |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |access-date=April 11, 2025 |work=The Guardian}}{{Cite news |last=Waterhouse |first=Benjamin C. |title=Donald Trump: Campaigns and Elections |url=https://millercenter.org/president/trump/campaigns-and-elections |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411113610/https://millercenter.org/president/trump/campaigns-and-elections |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |access-date=April 11, 2025 |work=[[Miller Center of Public Affairs]]}} As president, Trump lost the [[2020 United States presidential election|2020 presidential election]] to [[Democratic Party (United States)|Democratic]] nominee [[Joe Biden]].{{harvnb|Moniz|Swann|2025|p=1}} He and his allies in seven key states [[Big lie#Donald Trump's false claims of a stolen election|denied the results]]. They allegedly went on to devise a plot to create and submit fraudulent [[certificates of ascertainment]] falsely asserting that Trump had won the [[United States Electoral College|electoral college]] vote in those states.{{cite news |last1=Cohen |first1=Zachary |last2=Cohen |first2=Marshall |date=January 12, 2022 |title=Trump allies' fake Electoral College certificates offer fresh insights about plot to overturn Biden's victory |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/01/12/politics/trump-overturn-2020-election-fake-electoral-college/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220113003417/https://www.cnn.com/2022/01/12/politics/trump-overturn-2020-election-fake-electoral-college/index.html |archive-date=January 13, 2022 |access-date=July 24, 2022 |work=[[CNN]]}} In the event that the plot failed to "work out," Trump would plan another presidential run in 2024.{{Cite news |last1=Jackson |first1=David |last2=Fritze |first2=John |date=November 7, 2020 |title=What's Trump's next act after his loss? Would he run in 2024? Some advisers see it as a possibility |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2020/11/07/trump-run-2024-what-his-next-steps-after-loss-biden/6189382002/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211126191240/https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2020/11/07/trump-run-2024-what-his-next-steps-after-loss-biden/6189382002/ |archive-date=November 26, 2021 |access-date=November 12, 2020 |work=[[USA Today]]}}{{Cite news |author1=Bowden, John |author2=Woodward, Alex |author3=Baio, Ariana |date=November 5, 2020 |title=Can Trump run again in 2024? |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-election/trump-run-2024-election-biden-b1613385.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201214041257/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-election/trump-run-2024-election-biden-b1613385.html |archive-date=December 14, 2020 |access-date=November 12, 2020 |work=[[The Independent]]}}{{Cite news |last=Isenstadt |first=Alex |date=November 16, 2020 |title=4 more years: Trump freezes 2024 presidential field |url=https://www.politico.com/news/2020/11/16/trump-2024-presidential-field-436864 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201117010204/https://www.politico.com/news/2020/11/16/trump-2024-presidential-field-436864 |archive-date=November 17, 2020 |access-date=April 13, 2025 |work=[[Politico]]}} On January 6, 2021, a mob of Trump supporters [[January 6 United States Capitol attack|stormed the Capitol]] to prevent the true election results from being certified.{{harvnb|Pion-Berlin et al.|2022|pp=790–792}} The former President was thereafter [[Second impeachment of Donald Trump|impeached]] for incitement of [[insurrection]], but was [[Second impeachment trial of Donald Trump|acquitted]].{{cite news |last1=Naylor |first1=Brian |date=February 9, 2021 |title=Article of Impeachment Cites Trump's 'Incitement' of Capitol Insurrection |url=https://www.npr.org/sections/trump-impeachment-effort-live-updates/2021/01/11/955631105/impeachment-resolution-cites-trumps-incitement-of-capitol-insurrection |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210111193300/https://www.npr.org/sections/trump-impeachment-effort-live-updates/2021/01/11/955631105/impeachment-resolution-cites-trumps-incitement-of-capitol-insurrection |archive-date=January 11, 2021 |access-date=November 30, 2022 |work=[[NPR]]}} The [[Presidency of Joe Biden|Biden administration]] succeeding Trump's oversaw the end of the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID-19 pandemic]],{{Cite web |date=September 12, 2023 |title=End of the Federal COVID-19 Public Health Emergency (PHE) Declaration |url=https://archive.cdc.gov/www_cdc_gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/your-health/end-of-phe.html#:~:text=The%20federal%20COVID%2D19%20PHE,share%20certain%20data%20will%20change. |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250407182758/https://archive.cdc.gov/www_cdc_gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/your-health/end-of-phe.html#:~:text=The%20federal%20COVID%2D19%20PHE,share%20certain%20data%20will%20change. |archive-date=April 7, 2025 |access-date=April 13, 2025 |website=[[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention]]}} a [[2021–2023 inflation surge|spike in inflation lasting from 2021 to 2023]], a surge in crossings at the [[Mexico–United States border|border with Mexico]], and the outbreak of two major wars in [[Russian invasion of Ukraine|Ukraine]] and in [[Gaza war|Gaza]].{{Cite news |last=Zurcher |first=Anthony |date=January 13, 2025 |title=How Biden tarnished his own legacy |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c7vd5n3el6no |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250409151123/https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c7vd5n3el6no |archive-date=April 9, 2025 |access-date=April 13, 2025 |work=[[BBC News]]}}{{Cite news |last=Townley |first=Dafydd |date=January 13, 2025 |title=Joe Biden's legacy: four successes and four failures |url=https://theconversation.com/joe-bidens-legacy-four-successes-and-four-failures-246454 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250307180953/https://theconversation.com/joe-bidens-legacy-four-successes-and-four-failures-246454 |archive-date=March 7, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[The Conversation (website)|The Conversation]]}} While the President began his term with an [[Public image of Joe Biden|approval rating]] well above 50%, it had dropped to just 43% by September 2021, according to [[Gallup, Inc.|Gallup]], following the "chaotic"{{Cite news |last=Pengelly |first=Martin |date=February 16, 2024 |title=Biden 'privately defiant' over chaotic 2021 Afghanistan withdrawal, book says |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2024/feb/16/biden-afghanistan-withdrawal-book |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250122055323/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2024/feb/16/biden-afghanistan-withdrawal-book |archive-date=January 22, 2025 |access-date=April 13, 2025 |work=[[The Guardian]]}} [[Withdrawal of United States troops from Afghanistan|U.S. withdrawal from Afghanistan]] and a gradual rise in [[inflation]] from 1.7% in February to 5.4%.{{Cite web |title=Current US Inflation Rates: 2000-2025 |url=https://www.usinflationcalculator.com/inflation/current-inflation-rates/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250410081320/https://www.usinflationcalculator.com/inflation/current-inflation-rates/ |archive-date=April 10, 2025 |access-date=April 13, 2025 |website=US Inflation Calculator|date=July 23, 2008 }} His popularity never recovered, and by June 2022, inflation had risen to 9.1%, a 40-year high.{{Cite news |last1=Bayly |first1=Lucy |last2=Wallace |first2=Alicia |date=July 13, 2022 |title=US inflation hit 40-year high in June, driven by record gas prices |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2022/07/13/economy/cpi-inflation-june/index.html#:~:text=Inflation%20surged%20to%20a%20new,the%20Bureau%20of%20Labor%20Statistics. |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250401231223/https://edition.cnn.com/2022/07/13/economy/cpi-inflation-june/index.html#:~:text=Inflation%20surged%20to%20a%20new,the%20Bureau%20of%20Labor%20Statistics. |archive-date=April 1, 2025 |access-date=April 13, 2025 |work=[[CNN]]}} Besides a worsening economy, Biden oversaw a worsening in the [[Mexico–United States border crisis|crisis at the Southern border]], with 11.3 million undocumented immigrants entering the U.S. during his term.{{Cite web |last1=Chishti |first1=Muzaffar |last2=Bush-Joseph |first2=Kathleen |last3=Putzel-Kavanaugh |first3=Colleen |last4=Greene |first4=Madeleine |date=December 10, 2024 |title=Biden's Mixed Immigration Legacy: Border Challenges Overshadowed Modernization Advances |url=https://www.migrationpolicy.org/article/biden-immigration-legacy |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250404160146/https://www.migrationpolicy.org/article/biden-immigration-legacy |archive-date=April 4, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |website=[[Migration Policy Institute|Migration Information Source]]}} Ever since Russia invaded Ukraine in February 2022, Biden unwaveringly aided Ukraine. He sent the nation a total of $182 billion in emergency funding.{{Cite web |date=March 14, 2025 |title=How much money has the US given Ukraine since Russia's invasion? |url=https://usafacts.org/articles/how-much-money-has-the-us-given-ukraine-since-russias-invasion/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250407073550/https://usafacts.org/articles/how-much-money-has-the-us-given-ukraine-since-russias-invasion/ |archive-date=April 7, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |website=[[USAFacts]]}} When the Gaza war broke out in November 2023, the President strongly supported Israel.{{Cite news |last=Baker |first=Peter |date=October 10, 2023 |title=In Unforgiving Terms, Biden Condemns 'Evil' and 'Abhorrent' Attack on Israel |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/10/10/us/politics/biden-israel-hamas.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231012001950/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/10/10/us/politics/biden-israel-hamas.html |archive-date=October 12, 2023 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]]}} These three issues: global uncertainty, inflation, and the migrant crisis, would be the focal points of the future Trump campaign.{{Cite news |last=Goldmacher |first=Shane |date=December 5, 2024 |title=How Trump Targeted Undecided Voters Without Breaking the Bank |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/12/05/us/politics/trump-streaming-ads-strategy.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250424125812/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/12/05/us/politics/trump-streaming-ads-strategy.html |archive-date=April 24, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}} By July 2022, amid the [[public hearings of the United States House Select Committee on the January 6 Attack|public hearings of the House Select Committee on the January{{nbsp}}6 Attack]], Trump was reportedly considering making an early announcement of his 2024 candidacy.{{Cite news |last1=Bender |first1=Michael C. |last2=Epstein |first2=Reid J. |last3=Haberman |first3=Maggie |date=July 1, 2022 |title=Trump Eyes Early 2024 Announcement as Jan. 6 Scrutiny Intensifies |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/07/01/us/politics/trump-republicans-campaign-2024.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220703000859/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/07/01/us/politics/trump-republicans-campaign-2024.html |archive-date=July 3, 2022 |access-date=April 13, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]] |via=}}{{cite news |last1=Orr |first1=Gabby |last2=Holmes |first2=Kristen |last3=Zanona |first3=Melanie |date=July 2, 2022 |title=Trump weighs early 2024 launch as January 6 committee looms over his future |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/07/02/politics/trump-campaign-2024-republican/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220702165846/https://www.cnn.com/2022/07/02/politics/trump-campaign-2024-republican/index.html |archive-date=July 2, 2022 |access-date=July 2, 2022 |work=[[CNN]]}} A contemporary ''[[Intelligencer (website)|Intelligencer]]'' interview with Trump affirmed that he had already made up his mind.{{Cite news |last1=Nuzzi |first1=Olivia |date=July 14, 2022 |title=Donald Trump on 2024: 'I've Already Made That Decision' |url=https://nymag.com/intelligencer/article/donald-trump-2024-decision.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220721042249/https://nymag.com/intelligencer/article/donald-trump-2024-decision.html |archive-date=July 21, 2022 |access-date=July 16, 2022 |work=[[New York (magazine)|New York Intelligencer]]}} Following the August 2022 [[FBI search of Mar-a-Lago]], many of his allies urged that he initiate his campaign even sooner, perhaps prior to that year's [[2022 United States elections|midterm elections]].{{Cite news |last1=Orr |first1=Gabby |last2=Zanona |first2=Melanie |last3=Holmes |first3=Kristen |last4=Warren |first4=Michael |date=August 9, 2022 |title=Trump fields calls from Republican allies to speed up 2024 bid after FBI raid |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/08/09/politics/trump-2024-bid/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220811071430/https://www.cnn.com/2022/08/09/politics/trump-2024-bid/index.html |archive-date=August 11, 2022 |access-date=August 11, 2022 |work=[[CNN]]}} === Announcement === Donald Trump announced his candidacy for president on November{{nbsp}}15, 2022, in an hour-long address from [[Mar-a-Lago]]. It came one week after the midterm elections.{{cite news|last1=Chen |first1=Shawna |date=November 16, 2022 |title=Trump announces 2024 presidential campaign |url=https://www.axios.com/2022/11/16/donald-trump-2024-president-run |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221116015632/https://www.axios.com/2022/11/16/donald-trump-2024-president-run |archive-date=November 16, 2022 |access-date=November 16, 2022 |work=[[Axios (website)|Axios]]}}{{cite news |last1=Wilkie |first1=Christina |date=November 15, 2022 |title=Donald Trump announces his 2024 presidential campaign in a bid to seize early momentum |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2022/11/15/trump-news-trump-launches-2024-presidential-campaign-in-a-bid-to-seize-early-momentum.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221116015955/https://www.cnbc.com/2022/11/15/trump-news-trump-launches-2024-presidential-campaign-in-a-bid-to-seize-early-momentum.html |archive-date=November 16, 2022 |access-date=November 16, 2022 |work=[[CNBC]]}} The campaign would be based in [[Mar-a-Lago]], in [[West Palm Beach, Florida]].{{Cite news |last=Sczesny |first=Matt |date=November 17, 2022 |title=Mar-a-Lago will be base of operations for former President Trump's 2024 campaign |url=https://www.wptv.com/news/political/mar-a-lago-to-be-base-of-operations-for-former-president-trumps-2024-campaign |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241114181645/https://www.wptv.com/news/political/mar-a-lago-to-be-base-of-operations-for-former-president-trumps-2024-campaign |archive-date=November 14, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[WPTV-TV|WPTV]]}}{{Cite news |last=Watson |first=Kathryn |date=November 6, 2024 |title=Trump addresses supporters before clinching victory |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/donald-trump-election-night-watch-live-2024-11-06/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241226003207/https://www.cbsnews.com/news/donald-trump-election-night-watch-live-2024-11-06/ |archive-date=December 26, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[CBS News]]}} Reporting for [[Axios (website)|''Axios'']], Zachary Basu noted that at the time of the announcement, Trump was the "underdog" and "at the weakest moment of his political career".{{Cite news |last=Basu |first=Zachary |date=November 15, 2022 |title=Trump, the underdog |url=https://www.axios.com/2022/11/16/trump-2024-republican-underdog |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250427073933/https://www.axios.com/2022/11/16/trump-2024-republican-underdog |archive-date=April 27, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[Axios (website)|Axios]]}} His candidacy was met with a mixed response from both Democrats and Republicans. He was perceived by many as a weak, beatable candidate, owing to his loss in 2020 and the failure of an expected Republican "red wave" in the 2022 midterms to materialize.{{Efn|In fact, the Republican candidates Trump endorsed for the 2022 midterms generally underperformed those that he did not.{{cite news|first=Schlesinger|last=Robert|date=November 16, 2022|title=Apparently the Trump show must go on|url=https://www.nbcnews.com/think/opinion/donald-trump-2024-presidential-bid-built-on-alternative-facts-rcna57354|work=[[NBC News]]|access-date=April 11, 2025|archive-date=November 29, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129075126/https://www.nbcnews.com/think/opinion/donald-trump-2024-presidential-bid-built-on-alternative-facts-rcna57354|url-status=live}}|name=Roe v. Wade}} This led several Republican officials to oppose his campaign,{{cite news |last=McGreal |first=Chris |date=November 15, 2022 |title=Trump v DeSantis: Republicans split over 2024 run and predict 'blood on the floor' |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2022/nov/15/trump-v-desantis-republicans-divided-2024-presidential-election |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221201220624/https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2022/nov/15/trump-v-desantis-republicans-divided-2024-presidential-election |archive-date=December 1, 2022 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[The Guardian]]}}{{Cite news |last=Siders |first=David |date=November 27, 2022 |title=Republicans shrug off Trump '24 bid: 'The excitement's just not there' |url=https://www.politico.com/news/2022/11/27/republicans-against-trump-2024-00070632 |url-access=registration |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231003015722/https://www.politico.com/news/2022/11/27/republicans-against-trump-2024-00070632 |archive-date=October 3, 2023 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[Politico]]}}{{Cite news |last1=Manu |first1=Raju |last2=Zanona |first2=Melanie |last3=Barrett |first3=Ted |date=November 15, 2022 |title=Trump's 2024 bid gets harsh reaction among Hill Republicans |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2022/11/14/politics/trump-2024-harsh-reaction-capitol-hill-republicans/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250116231734/https://edition.cnn.com/2022/11/14/politics/trump-2024-harsh-reaction-capitol-hill-republicans/index.html |archive-date=January 16, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[CNN]]}} and several Democrats to welcome it.{{Cite news |last1=Hunnicutt |first1=Trevor |last2=Renshaw |first2=Jarrett |date=November 17, 2022 |title=Biden's team warily welcomes Trump's 2024 presidential run |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/us/bidens-team-warily-welcomes-trumps-2024-presidential-run-2022-11-16/ |url-access=registration |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221202063820/https://www.reuters.com/world/us/bidens-team-warily-welcomes-trumps-2024-presidential-run-2022-11-16/ |archive-date=December 2, 2022 |access-date=December 2, 2022 |work=[[Reuters]] |language=en}}{{Cite news |last=Cillizza |first=Chris |date=November 15, 2022 |title=Democrats are openly rooting for another Trump presidential bid |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2022/11/15/politics/democrats-trump-2024-presidential-bid/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241110045911/https://edition.cnn.com/2022/11/15/politics/democrats-trump-2024-presidential-bid/index.html |archive-date=November 10, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[CNN]]}} The [[Conservatism in the United States|conservative]] ''[[New York Post]]'' mocked Trump's announcement by relegating it to page 26 and noting it on the cover with a banner reading "[[Florida Man]] Makes Announcement".{{Cite news |last=Stableford |first=Dylan |date=November 16, 2022 |title=New York Post buries Trump 2024 launch: 'Florida Man Makes Announcement' |url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/new-york-post-trump-florida-man-makes-announcement-162902923.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250413223139/https://www.yahoo.com/news/new-york-post-trump-florida-man-makes-announcement-162902923.html?guccounter=1&guce_referrer=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ29vZ2xlLmNvbS8&guce_referrer_sig=AQAAABasao_v5hejavCm6qAF-k6UrruWgJI_wtVgs39APlV7eh7VFR6h2TiCh048GbLeEJTT9tJYEA97tYMSwc7qcPx5oEHwgzanvFbU7xY8_K4QRqPDsUlf9E9w1il0hqXKM1KcMcH8v1-PDkSFQn8hAaixp_edhyZL9p5pgMW3dLvG |archive-date=April 13, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[Yahoo! News]]}} On the other hand, Trump-aligned Republicans embraced the campaign,{{Cite news |last=Coppins |first=McKay |date=2023-01-30 |title=Republicans' 2024 Magical Thinking |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2023/01/2024-republican-primary-donald-trump-deus-ex-machina/672888/ |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230130195122/https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2023/01/2024-republican-primary-donald-trump-deus-ex-machina/672888/ |archive-date=January 30, 2023 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[The Atlantic]]}} and many Democrats deemed it a threat to American [[democracy]].{{Cite news |author=The New York Times Editorial Board |date=November 16, 2022 |title=America Deserves Better Than Donald Trump |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/11/15/opinion/trump-2024-announcement.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221201235333/https://www.nytimes.com/2022/11/15/opinion/trump-2024-announcement.html |archive-date=December 1, 2022 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}}{{Cite news |last=Tamkin |first=Emily |date=November 16, 2022 |title=What will Donald Trump's 2024 presidential bid mean for American democracy? |url=https://www.newstatesman.com/world/americas/north-america/us/2022/11/donald-trump-announcement-2024-presidential-campaign |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221202212032/https://www.newstatesman.com/world/americas/north-america/us/2022/11/donald-trump-announcement-2024-presidential-campaign |archive-date=December 2, 2022 |access-date=December 2, 2022 |website=[[New Statesman]]}} Trump was the first one-term president to campaign for a second non-consecutive term since [[Herbert Hoover]] (1929–1933), who, after losing in [[1932 United States presidential election|1932]], made unsuccessful runs in [[1936 United States presidential election|1936]] and [[1940 United States presidential election|1940]].{{cite news|last1=Baker|first1=Peter|title=Trump Hints at Another Act in Four Years, Just Like Grover Cleveland|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/12/02/us/politics/trump-2024.html|access-date=April 12, 2025|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|date=December 2, 2020|archive-date=February 9, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210209183734/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/12/02/us/politics/trump-2024.html|url-status=live|url-access=subscription}} === Fundraising === At its inception, Trump's campaign had over $100 million in funding.{{cite news |last=Schouten |first=Fredreka |date=November 16, 2022 |title=Questions about Donald Trump's campaign money, answered |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/11/16/politics/donald-trump-war-chest-presidential-campaign/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241205102952/https://edition.cnn.com/2022/11/16/politics/donald-trump-war-chest-presidential-campaign/index.html |archive-date=December 5, 2024 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[CNN]]}} Its primary vehicle for fundraising was [[Save America]], a [[Political action committee#Leadership PACs|leadership political action committee]] (PAC), joined by the MAGA PAC and Super PAC.{{Cite news |last=Wolf |first=Zachary B. |date=June 5, 2024 |title=How are Trump's legal bills and the 2024 campaigns being funded? There's a lot we don't know |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2024/06/05/politics/donald-trump-legal-bills-what-matters/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240902183053/https://edition.cnn.com/2024/06/05/politics/donald-trump-legal-bills-what-matters/index.html |archive-date=September 2, 2024 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[CNN]]}}{{cite news |last1=Escobar |first1=Molly Cook |last2=Sun |first2=Albert |last3=Goldmacher |first3=Shane |date=March 27, 2024 |title=How Trump Moved Money to Pay $100 Million in Legal Bills |url=https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2024/03/27/us/politics/trump-cases-legal-fund.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250404150558/https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2024/03/27/us/politics/trump-cases-legal-fund.html |archive-date=April 4, 2025 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}} However, his legal expenses from his court cases would absorb much of that funding. In fact, from January 2021 to March 2024, he spent more than $100 million in legal fees from campaign accounts. In 2023, the year of Trump's four criminal indictments, over half of his financial donations were allocated to paying off legal bills. While running against Joe Biden, Trump overwhelmingly lagged behind his opponent in fundraising. His legal expenses combined with Biden's plentiful financial hauls laid at the heart of this problem.{{cite news |last=Weissert |first=Will |date=January 15, 2024 |title=Biden and the Democrats raise $97 million to close out 2023 after a December fundraising blitz |url=https://apnews.com/article/election-2024-biden-democrats-fundraising-5923a86caf124b9c789276fe3349d42f |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240330190451/https://apnews.com/article/election-2024-biden-democrats-fundraising-5923a86caf124b9c789276fe3349d42f |archive-date=March 30, 2024 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[Associated Press]]}} At the start of March 2024, Trump's campaign and Trump-aligned [[Super PAC]]s had half as much cash on hand as Biden's campaign and Biden-aligned Super PACs.{{cite news |last=Levitz |first=Eric |date=March 29, 2024 |title=Why Biden's fundraising dominance could save his campaign |url=https://www.vox.com/2024-elections/24115423/biden-fundraiser-trump-clinton-obama-2024-polls |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240921164306/https://www.vox.com/2024-elections/24115423/biden-fundraiser-trump-clinton-obama-2024-polls |archive-date=September 21, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[Vox (website)|Vox]]}} However, Trump's fundraising eventually took a turn for the better, with the former President raising more money than his opponent in April, and beating Biden's total fundraising for the first time.{{Cite news |last1=Davis O'Brien |first1=Rebecca |last2=Goldmacher |first2=Shane |date=May 21, 2024 |title=Trump's Reported Fund-Raising Tops Biden's for First Time |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/05/21/us/politics/trump-biden-fundraising.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240523192826/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/05/21/us/politics/trump-biden-fundraising.html |archive-date=May 23, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]]}} Things again turned sour for Trump's campaign after Biden withdrew from the race. The new Democratic nominee, [[Kamala Harris]], brought in $200 million during the first week of her presidential campaign.{{Cite news |last=Bressner |first=Noah |date=July 29, 2024 |title=Trump's falling legal fees free up campaign cash |url=https://www.axios.com/2024/07/29/trump-falling-legal-costs-fundraising |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240921213857/https://www.axios.com/2024/07/29/trump-falling-legal-costs-fundraising |archive-date=September 21, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[Axios (website)|Axios]]}} In July, Trump's campaign and assorted committees reporting taking in $138.7 million compared to Harris and Democratic committees' $310 million.{{Cite news |last=Weissert |first=Will |date=August 2, 2024 |title=Harris raised a massive $310 million in July, as she looks to reset November's race against Trump |url=https://apnews.com/article/harris-fundraising-democrats-trump-fbc14aa926444b4f961f579c63811766 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240806041835/https://apnews.com/article/harris-fundraising-democrats-trump-fbc14aa926444b4f961f579c63811766 |archive-date=August 6, 2024 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[Associated Press]]}} All in all, throughout their campaigns (specifically, since January 2023), the Trump committee raised $388 million, while that Biden–Harris raised nearly $1 billion.{{Cite news |last=Durkee |first=Alison |date=November 4, 2024 |title=Trump Vs. Harris Fundraising: Harris Outraises Trump By Nearly 5-to-1 Among Last Minute Big Donors |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/alisondurkee/2024/11/04/trump-vs-harris-fundraising-race-harris-outraised-trump-3-to-1-with-last-pre-election-report/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250404235918/https://www.forbes.com/sites/alisondurkee/2024/11/04/trump-vs-harris-fundraising-race-harris-outraised-trump-3-to-1-with-last-pre-election-report/ |archive-date=April 4, 2025 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[Forbes]]}} According to [[OpenSecrets]], Trump's greatest donors were [[hedge fund]] manager Ken Griffin (who donated $100 million), pro-[[Israel]] activist [[Miriam Adelson]] ($132 million), railroad magnate [[Timothy Mellon]] ($197 million), and, most notably, businessman [[Elon Musk]] ($277 million{{Efn|While estimates of Musk's campaign donations widely vary, all major sources put them above $250 million.{{cite news|last1=Schouten|first1=Fredreka|last2=Wright|first2=David|last3=Matthews|first3=Alex Leeds|date=December 6, 2024|title=Musk spent more than a quarter-billion dollars to elect Trump, including funding a mysterious super PAC, new filings show |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2024/12/05/politics/elon-musk-trump-campaign-finance-filings/index.html|work=[[CNN]]|url-status=live|access-date=April 12, 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250302173714/https://edition.cnn.com/2024/12/05/politics/elon-musk-trump-campaign-finance-filings/index.html|archive-date=March 2, 2025}}{{cite news|last=Hawkinson|first=Katie|date=February 2, 2025|title=Elon Musk dropped nearly $300M supporting Donald Trump in 2024 |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/elon-musk-donald-trump-2024-election-b2690735.html|work=[[The Independent]]|url-status=live|access-date=April 12, 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250330010700/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/elon-musk-donald-trump-2024-election-b2690735.html|archive-date=March 30, 2025}}{{cite news|last=Lim|first=Clarissa-Jan|date=December 6, 2024|title=Here's how much money Elon Musk spent to help Trump win the election |url=https://www.msnbc.com/top-stories/latest/elon-musk-trump-donations-2024-election-rcna183231|work=[[MSNBC]]|url-status=live|access-date=April 12, 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250406141704/https://www.msnbc.com/top-stories/latest/elon-musk-trump-donations-2024-election-rcna183231|archive-date=April 6, 2025}}}}).{{Cite news |last=Klaidman |first=Daniel |date=November 14, 2024 |title=The megadonors who fueled Trump and the Republican Party and what they may want in the next 4 years |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/trump-megadonors-2024-election/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250405010415/https://www.cbsnews.com/news/trump-megadonors-2024-election/ |archive-date=April 5, 2025 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[CBS News]]}} Musk was not only the largest individual political donor of the 2024 election, but also the largest individual political donor since at least 2010, excluding candidates funding their own campaigns. He also launched a $1 million a day giveaway for swing state voters.{{Cite news |last1=Thadani |first1=Trisha |last2=Morse |first2=Clara Ence |date=December 6, 2024 |title=Elon Musk is now America's largest political donor |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/technology/2024/12/06/elon-musk-trump-campaign-spending-fec/ |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250414073209/https://www.washingtonpost.com/technology/2024/12/06/elon-musk-trump-campaign-spending-fec/ |archive-date=April 14, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]}} OpenSecrets additionally found that the top seven donors of the 2024 campaign were "solidly Republican/Conservative".{{Cite web |last=Meyers |first=David |date=March 26, 2025 |title=Elon Musk tops list of 2024 political donors, but five others gave more than $100 million |url=https://www.opensecrets.org/news/2025/03/elon-musk-tops-list-of-2024-political-donors-but-six-others-gave-more-than-100-million |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250404173903/https://www.opensecrets.org/news/2025/03/elon-musk-tops-list-of-2024-political-donors-but-six-others-gave-more-than-100-million/ |archive-date=April 4, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |website=[[OpenSecrets]]}} Trump notably mixed his personal business with political fundraising.{{cite news |last1=Breuninger |first1=Kevin |date=March 29, 2024 |title=Trump's deals to sell Bibles, sneakers and perfume are unprecedented for a presidential candidate, experts say |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2024/03/29/trump-sellig-bibles-sneakers-perfume-on-campaign-trail-unprecedented-experts-say.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250320105246/https://www.cnbc.com/2024/03/29/trump-sellig-bibles-sneakers-perfume-on-campaign-trail-unprecedented-experts-say.html |archive-date=March 20, 2025 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[CNBC]]}} He promoted $59.99 [[God Bless the U.S.A. Bible|"God Bless the U.S.A." Bibles]], $399 sneakers, $99 "Victory47" cologne, and $99 Trump-branded [[Non-fungible tokens|NFT]] digital trading cards for his personal, non-campaign accounts.{{cite news |last1=Kim |first1=Soo Rin |last2=Ibssa |first2=Lalee |date=March 28, 2024 |title=Trump endorses line of Bibles -- after selling shoes, NFTs and more |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/trump-endorses-line-bibles-after-selling-shoes-nfts/story?id=108543985 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240329174452/https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/trump-endorses-line-bibles-after-selling-shoes-nfts/story?id=108543985 |archive-date=March 29, 2024 |access-date=April 12, 2025 |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]]}} Many campaign funds were also funneled into Trump-owned businesses, in particular his [[Mar-a-Lago]] resort and the [[Trump National Doral Miami]].{{cite news |last1=Anderson |first1=Zac |last2=Mansfield |first2=Erin |date=April 18, 2024 |title=Trump is funneling campaign money into cash-strapped businesses. Experts say it looks bad. |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2024/04/18/trump-campaign-funnels-money-to-his-businesses/73344744007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240420053434/https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2024/04/18/trump-campaign-funnels-money-to-his-businesses/73344744007/ |archive-date=April 20, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[USA Today]]}} === Eligibility === {{main|Presidential eligibility of Donald Trump|Trump v. Anderson}} [[File:Trump 2024 state ballot eligibility map.svg|thumb|Republican primary ballot eligibility prior to ''[[Trump v. Anderson]]'', the U.S. Supreme Court that established Trump's eligibility {{legend|#00bb00|Case dismissed by state supreme court}} {{legend|#90EE90|Case dismissed by lower court}} {{legend|#cc9933|Decision ruled that Trump is ineligible; stayed, pending appeal}} {{legend|#666666|Lawsuit filed}}]]Trump's eligibility to run for president was challenged. The [[Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution|Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution]], [[Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution#Section 3: Disqualification from office for insurrection or rebellion|Section 3]], prohibits current and former federal, state and military officials who have "engaged in insurrection or rebellion" from holding office again, which was pertinent in Trump's case considering his role in inciting the [[January 6 United States Capitol attack|January 6 attack on the Capitol]].{{Cite news |last1=Cabral |first1=Sam |last2=Epstein |first2=Kayla |date=February 8, 2024 |title=The 14th Amendment plan to disqualify Trump, explained |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-66690276 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250403124948/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-66690276 |archive-date=April 3, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[BBC News]]}}{{Cite web |last=Bomboy |first=Scott |date=February 5, 2024 |title=Explaining Donald Trump's 14th Amendment case at the Supreme Court |url=https://constitutioncenter.org/blog/explaining-donald-trumps-14th-amendment-case-at-the-supreme-court |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250315040521/https://constitutioncenter.org/blog/explaining-donald-trumps-14th-amendment-case-at-the-supreme-court |archive-date=March 15, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |website=[[National Constitution Center]]}} By 2023, the non-profit group [[Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington]] and other advocacy groups and individuals were planning state-by-state efforts to keep Trump off state ballots.{{cite news |last1=Demissie |first1=Hannah |last2=Gersony |first2=Laura |date=August 26, 2023 |title=14th Amendment, Section 3: A new legal battle against Trump takes shape |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/14th-amendment-section-3-new-legal-battle-trump/story?id=102547316 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250319002336/https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/14th-amendment-section-3-new-legal-battle-trump/story?id=102547316 |archive-date=March 19, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]]}} Court cases sprung up in multiple states.{{cite news |last1=Williams |first1=Corey |date=November 17, 2023 |title=Group asks Michigan Supreme Court to hear an appeal of a ruling in Trump ballot case |url=https://apnews.com/article/trump-insurrection-14th-amendment-ballot-michigan-43f3b5bc379a2a1de66d21101252e40f |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241113010748/https://apnews.com/article/trump-insurrection-14th-amendment-ballot-michigan-43f3b5bc379a2a1de66d21101252e40f |archive-date=November 13, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[Associated Press]]}} In December 2023, the [[Colorado Supreme Court]] [[Trump v. Anderson|ruled]] that, under the Fourteenth Amendment, Trump was ineligible from holding office and that his name must be removed from the Colorado Republican primary ballot.{{Cite news |last1=Halpert |first1=Madeline |last2=Drenon |first2=Brandon |date=December 20, 2023 |title=Colorado Supreme Court kicks Trump off ballot, citing 'insurrection' |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-67768873 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250329210145/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-67768873 |archive-date=March 29, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[BBC News]]}} This decision was the first of its kind in American history.{{Cite news |last=Quinn |first=Melissa |date=December 21, 2023 |title=Colorado Supreme Court rules Trump is disqualified from presidency for Jan. 6 riot |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/trump-colorado-supreme-court/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250307193643/https://www.cbsnews.com/news/trump-colorado-supreme-court/ |archive-date=March 7, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[CBS News]] |language=en-US}} Later that month, [[Shenna Bellows|Maine's Secretary of State]] followed suit and banned Trump from Maine's Republican primary ballot. In March 2024, following an appeal from Trump's campaign,{{Cite news |last=Astor |first=Maggie |date=March 4, 2024 |title=Trump Is Disqualified From 2024 Ballot, Colorado Court Says in Explosive Ruling |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/12/19/us/politics/trump-colorado-ballot-14th-amendment.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250402092714/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/12/19/us/politics/trump-colorado-ballot-14th-amendment.html |archive-date=April 2, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}} the U.S. Supreme Court unanimously [[Trump v. Anderson|overturned]] Colorado's Supreme Court ruling, saying that states do not have the authority to disqualify Trump or other candidates from federal elections under the Fourteenth Amendment's insurrection clause.{{cite news |last=Riccardi |first=Nicholas |date=March 4, 2024 |title=Takeaways from Trump's Supreme Court win: He stays on ballot, but his legal peril is just starting |url=https://apnews.com/article/supreme-court-trump-colorado-ballot-2390f3204e3ecaad3c617f9db0ff9d2e |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250116210044/https://apnews.com/article/supreme-court-trump-colorado-ballot-2390f3204e3ecaad3c617f9db0ff9d2e |archive-date=January 16, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[Associated Press]]}} ==Agenda and strategy== {{see also|Agenda 47|Project 2025}} === Agenda === Donald Trump's formal campaign manifesto was [[Agenda 47]]. It took the form of a series of videos on his official website outlining his proposals one by one.{{Cite news |last=Allen |first=Mike |date=2024-03-06 |title=Scoop: Biden campaign's plan of attack |url=https://www.axios.com/2024/03/06/biden-trump-project-2025 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240421061054/https://www.axios.com/2024/03/06/biden-trump-project-2025 |archive-date=April 21, 2024 |access-date=April 11, 2025 |work=[[Axios (website)|Axios]]}} Seeing that the series was cut short in December 2023, Agenda 47 was primarily targeted to Republican voters during the [[2024 Republican Party presidential primaries|2024 primary season]].{{Cite news |last=Durkee |first=Alison |date=January 17, 2025 |title=What Is Agenda47? What To Know About Trump's Policy Agenda Before His Inauguration |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/alisondurkee/2025/01/17/what-is-agenda47-what-to-know-about-trumps-policy-agenda-before-his-inauguration/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411161756/https://www.forbes.com/sites/alisondurkee/2025/01/17/what-is-agenda47-what-to-know-about-trumps-policy-agenda-before-his-inauguration/ |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |newspaper=[[Forbes]]}}{{Cite news |last=Bump |first=Philip |date=June 18, 2024 |title=Trump has unveiled an agenda of his own. He just doesn't mention it much |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2024/06/18/trump-has-unveiled-an-agenda-his-own-he-just-doesnt-mention-it-much/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240628085237/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2024/06/18/trump-has-unveiled-an-agenda-his-own-he-just-doesnt-mention-it-much/ |archive-date=June 28, 2024 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]}} His website's homepage contained a list of 20 campaign proposals. {{Collapsible list | title = President Trump's 20 CORE PROMISES TO MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!{{cite web|date=November 4, 2024|title=Agenda 47|url=https://www.donaldjtrump.com/platform/|website=Donald J. Trump|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241104084915/https://www.donaldjtrump.com/platform/ |access-date=April 11, 2025|archive-date=November 4, 2024 |url-status=dead}} | bullets = true|Seal the [[Mexico-U.S. border|border]] and stop the migrant invasion|Carry out the largest deportation operation in American history|End [[inflation]], and make America affordable again|Make America the dominant energy producer in the world, by far|Stop [[outsourcing]], and turn the United States into a manufacturing superpower|Large tax cuts for workers, and no tax on tips|Defend our [[Constitution of the United States|Constitution]], our [[United States Bill of Rights|Bill of Rights]], and our fundamental freedoms, including [[Freedom of speech in the United States|freedom of speech]], [[Freedom of religion in the United States|freedom of religion]], and the [[right to keep and bear arms]]|Prevent [[World War Three]], restore peace in Europe and in the Middle East, and build a great iron dome missile defense shield over our entire country—all made in America|End the weaponization of government against the American people|Stop the migrant crime epidemic, demolish the foreign [[drug cartels]], crush gang violence, and lock up violent offenders|Rebuild our cities, including [[Washington, D.C.]], making them safe, clean, and beautiful again|Strengthen and modernize our [[United States Armed Forces|military]], making it, without question, the strongest and most powerful in the world|Keep the [[U.S. dollar]] as the world's [[reserve currency]]|Fight for and protect [[Social Security (United States)|Social Security]] and [[Medicare (United States)|Medicare]] with no cuts, including no changes to the [[retirement age]]|Cancel the [[electric vehicle]] mandate and cut costly and burdensome regulations|Cut federal funding for any school pushing [[critical race theory]], radical gender ideology, and other inappropriate racial, sexual, or political content on our children|Keep men out of women's sports|Deport pro-[[Hamas]] [[Gaza war protests in the United States|radicals]] and make our college campuses safe and patriotic again|Secure our elections, including same day voting, [[Voter identification laws in the United States|voter identification]], paper ballots, and proof of citizenship|Unite our country by bringing it to new and record levels of success }} According to Philip Bump of ''[[The Washington Post]]'', Agenda 47 was rarely discussed by Trump as well as the media. He, and others, noted that it was overshadowed by another presidential transition plan closely tied to—in fact, designed for—the Trump campaign, [[The Heritage Foundation|The Heritage Foundation's]] [[Project 2025]].{{cite news |last1=Arnsdorf |first1=Isaac |last2=Dawsey |first2=Josh |last3=Barrett |first3=Devlin |date=November 6, 2023 |title=Trump and allies plot revenge, Justice Department control in a second term |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2023/11/05/trump-revenge-second-term/ |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231105234529/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2023/11/05/trump-revenge-second-term/ |archive-date=November 5, 2023 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]}}{{cite news |last=Stone |first=Peter |date=November 22, 2023 |title='Openly authoritarian campaign': Trump's threats of revenge fuel alarm |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2023/nov/22/trump-revenge-game-plan-alarm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231127003512/https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2023/nov/22/trump-revenge-game-plan-alarm |archive-date=November 27, 2023 |access-date=April 11, 2025 |work=[[The Guardian]]}} It planned for massive overhauls to American government, steering it in an uncompromisingly [[Conservatism in the United States|conservative]] path and relegating much authority to the [[Executive (government)|executive branch]]. As such, Project 2025 was condemned for unconstitutionally encouraging [[authoritarianism]] and moving to turn Trump into a dictator.{{cite news |last=Moynihan |first=Donald |date=November 27, 2023 |title=Trump Has a Master Plan for Destroying the 'Deep State' |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/11/27/opinion/trump-deep-state-schedule-f.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231127152541/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/11/27/opinion/trump-deep-state-schedule-f.html |archive-date=November 27, 2023 |access-date=April 15, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}}{{cite news |last1=O'Neill |first1=Joseph |date=March 21, 2023 |title=One Man's Foray Into the Heartland of the Far Right |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/03/21/books/review/the-undertow-jeff-sharlet.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240326084955/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/03/21/books/review/the-undertow-jeff-sharlet.html |archive-date=March 26, 2024 |access-date=April 15, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}}{{cite news |last=Ackerman |first=Spencer |date=August 3, 2023 |title=This Is How Trump Becomes a Dictator |url=https://www.thenation.com/article/politics/trump-presidential-power-security-state/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230907062254/https://www.thenation.com/article/politics/trump-presidential-power-security-state/ |archive-date=September 7, 2023 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[The Nation]]}}{{Cite news |last1=Swan |first1=Jonathan |last2=Savage |first2=Charlie |last3=Haberman |first3=Maggie |date=July 17, 2023 |title=Trump and Allies Forge Plans to Increase Presidential Power in 2025 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/07/17/us/politics/trump-plans-2025.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250409160327/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/07/17/us/politics/trump-plans-2025.html |archive-date=April 9, 2025 |access-date=April 15, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}} Trump's campaign officials repeatedly distanced themselves from the plan, stressing that all outside efforts influencing a future presidential transition were "unofficial". Trump himself denied knowing of Project 2025. He went as far as to call some of its proposals "absolutely ridiculous" and "seriously extreme".{{Cite news |last=Gleeson |first=Cailey |title=Trump Disavows Project 2025: Calls Some Of Conservative Group's Ideas 'Absolutely Ridiculous And Abysmal' |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/caileygleeson/2024/07/05/trump-disavows-project-2025-calls-some-of-conservative-groups-ideas-absolutely-ridiculous-and-abysmal/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240709020506/https://www.forbes.com/sites/caileygleeson/2024/07/05/trump-disavows-project-2025-calls-some-of-conservative-groups-ideas-absolutely-ridiculous-and-abysmal/ |archive-date=July 9, 2024 |access-date=2024-07-22 |website=[[Forbes]] |language=en}}{{Cite news |last1=Cappelletti |first1=Joey |last2=Colvin |first2=Jill |date=July 21, 2024 |title=Trump holds first rally after assassination attempt with his new running mate, Vance, by his side |url=https://apnews.com/article/trump-michigan-grand-rapids-vance-dcc7969cd53e281e32beb72a2c6e0fe8 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240723000442/https://apnews.com/article/trump-michigan-grand-rapids-vance-dcc7969cd53e281e32beb72a2c6e0fe8 |archive-date=July 23, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[Associated Press]] |language=en}} Besides The Heritage Foundation, other think-tanks and policy groups aligned with Trump included the [[Center for Renewing America]], the [[America First Policy Institute]], and [[America First Legal]]. Trump's preeminent public policy advisers were [[Steve Bannon]], [[David Bernhardt]], [[Kellyanne Conway]], [[Richard Grenell]], [[Tom Homan]], [[Sean Hannity]], [[Kevin Hassett]], [[Brandon Judd]], [[Keith Kellogg]], [[Larry Kudlow]], [[Robert Lighthizer]], [[Stephen Miller (political advisor)|Stephen Miller]], [[Stephen Moore (writer)|Stephen Moore]], [[John Ratcliffe (American politician)|John Ratcliffe]], [[Russell Vought]], and [[Matthew Whitaker|Matt Whitaker]], though none of them were formally part of the campaign itself.{{cite magazine |last=Cortellessa |first=Eric |date=April 30, 2024 |title=How Far Trump Would Go |url=https://time.com/6972021/donald-trump-2024-election-interview/ |url-status=live campaign’s policy proposals. Murtaugh, senior adviser|Alex Pfeiffer, senior adviser|[[Brian Jack]], senior aide, focusing on amassing endorsements from lawmakers|[[Dan Scavino]], senior aide }} ===Strategy=== ==== Political ==== Trump attempted to build a broad demographic coalition consisting of [[Hispanic and Latino Americans|Latinos]], [[Arab Americans]], [[Black people|Black men]], and young men—all groups that traditionally leaned [[Democratic Party (United States)|Democratic]]. He spoke sharply against the economy under [[Presidency of Joe Biden|Biden's presidency]], which resonated with all groups, and stoked [[culture war]] issues, appealing to Black and Latino men, who tend toward [[Social conservatism in the United States|social conservatism]].{{Cite web |last=Chatburn |first=Ross |date=November 20, 2024 |title=The Trump coalition that won him back the White House |url=https://collegegreengroup.com/blogs/us-politics/the-trump-coalition-that-won-him-back-the-white-house/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250430161334/https://collegegreengroup.com/blogs/us-politics/the-trump-coalition-that-won-him-back-the-white-house/ |archive-date=April 30, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |website=College Green Group}}{{Cite news |last=Karma |first=Rogé |date=November 8, 2024 |title=The Strategist Who Predicted Trump's Multiracial Coalition |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2024/11/trump-black-latino-voters-interview/680588/ |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250415153249/https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2024/11/trump-black-latino-voters-interview/680588/ |archive-date=April 15, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[The Atlantic]]}} According to [[Patrick Ruffini]], the former President stood to capitalize off of a gradual political phenomenon: that "the ties that once bound low-income and nonwhite voters to the Democratic Party ... were breaking". Trump also visited cities with concentrated Arab American populations. His efforts were bolstered by the Biden administration's pro-Israel stance on the [[Gaza war]], and [[Kamala Harris]]' neglect of these cities throughout the campaign.{{Cite news |last=Abdelrahman |first=Abdelhalim |date=November 13, 2024 |title=Why Michigan's Arab Americans Voted for Trump |url=https://foreignpolicy.com/2024/11/13/michigan-dearborn-trump-harris-arab-gaza-israel-vote/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250401013422/https://foreignpolicy.com/2024/11/13/michigan-dearborn-trump-harris-arab-gaza-israel-vote/ |archive-date=April 1, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[Foreign Policy]]}} To drive up turnout, Trump's campaign ran an unconventional [[Grassroots|ground game]]. He targeted irregular voters through community building, rather than traditional methods: door knocks, big party machinery, and paid media.{{Cite news |last1=Contorno |first1=Steve |last2=Schouten |first2=Fredreka |date=October 4, 2024 |title=Trump's ground game relies on untraditional strategies to draw out battleground voters |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2024/10/04/politics/trump-campaign-ground-game/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250501000551/https://edition.cnn.com/2024/10/04/politics/trump-campaign-ground-game/index.html |archive-date=May 1, 2025 |access-date=May 1, 2025 |work=[[CNN]]}}{{Cite news |last=Taylor |first=Astra |date=November 25, 2024 |title=Democrats should stop mocking Trump's ground game and start learning from it |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/ng-interactive/2024/nov/25/democrats-organizing-get-out-the-vote |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250401012512/https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/ng-interactive/2024/nov/25/democrats-organizing-get-out-the-vote |archive-date=April 1, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[The Guardian]]}} Organizations such as Libre and [[Turning Point USA]], besides driving forward an ideological agenda, assembled low propensity voters who felt alienated by the government and cultivated in them a sense of belonging to Trump's cause. As activist [[Tory Gavito|Tony Gavito]] explained, "Mobilizing people to turn out and cast a ballot is not nearly as powerful as organizing people to adopt an identity, commit to a cause, and join a collective effort to push for change". Regarding rhetoric, Trump deployed fiery, partisan language that, according to commentators, alienated the general public. He rejected the traditional pivot to the center and relied on negative messaging. Even in the campaign's final weeks, he continued homing in on his base and steadfast conservatives, while his opponent, Harris, tried appealing to moderates. This was done to "maximize turnout" from Trump's base.{{Cite news |last1=Colvin |first1=Jill |last2=Barrow |first2=Bill |date=March 4, 2024 |title=Trump keeps making incendiary statements. His campaign says that won't change |url=https://apnews.com/article/trump-biden-republicans-pivot-e1d9ff3444ce5c7cc942250cff057be5 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250406071021/https://apnews.com/article/trump-biden-republicans-pivot-e1d9ff3444ce5c7cc942250cff057be5 |archive-date=April 6, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[Associated Press]]}}{{Cite news |last1=Peoples |first1=Steve |last2=Colvin |first2=Jill |last3=Miller |first3=Zeke |date=October 23, 2024 |title=Trump targets hardcore partisans, Harris goes after moderates: Inside the campaign's final sprint |url=https://apnews.com/article/trump-harris-campaign-strategy-final-days-623cf25b673d7e875e69a904b93e8958 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411191950/https://apnews.com/article/trump-harris-campaign-strategy-final-days-623cf25b673d7e875e69a904b93e8958 |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[Associated Press]]}}{{Cite news |last=Smolar |first=Piotr |date=September 15, 2024 |title=2024 US election: Donald Trump's strange strategy of ignoring moderates |url=https://www.lemonde.fr/en/opinion/article/2024/09/15/2024-us-election-donald-trump-s-strange-strategy-of-ignoring-moderates_6726128_23.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250414012536/https://www.lemonde.fr/en/opinion/article/2024/09/15/2024-us-election-donald-trump-s-strange-strategy-of-ignoring-moderates_6726128_23.html |archive-date=April 14, 2025 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[Le Monde]]}} Beyond his base, it served to persuade remaining undecided voters; with extreme rhetoric, they would have a "compelling reason to vote".{{Cite news |last=Goldberg |first=Jonah |date=October 1, 2024 |title=Column: The political strategy behind Donald Trump's increasingly dark and disturbing rhetoric |url=https://www.latimes.com/opinion/story/2024-10-01/donald-trump-kamala-harris-immigration-mentally-disabled-election-2024-jonah-goldberg |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241225003429/https://www.latimes.com/opinion/story/2024-10-01/donald-trump-kamala-harris-immigration-mentally-disabled-election-2024-jonah-goldberg |archive-date=December 25, 2024 |access-date=April 30, 2025 |work=[[The Los Angeles Times]]}} Trump's extreme statements also played into his populist strategy of airing the public's grievances against the political status quo—that he was saying what no other politician dared to say.{{Cite news |last1=Goldmacher |first1=Shane |last2=Haberman |first2=Maggie |last3=Swan |first3=Jonathan |date=November 16, 2024 |title=How Trump Won, and How Harris Lost |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/11/07/us/politics/trump-win-election-harris.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250409104254/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/11/07/us/politics/trump-win-election-harris.html |archive-date=April 9, 2025 |access-date=April 14, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}}{{Cite web |last1=Chouliaraki |first1=Lilie |last2=Higgins |first2=Kathryn Claire |date=January 22, 2025 |title=Trump rode pain and victimhood to power, but grievance may not be an effective basis for governing |url=https://blogs.lse.ac.uk/usappblog/2025/01/22/trump-rode-pain-and-victimhood-to-power-but-grievance-may-not-be-a-effective-basis-for-governing/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250430220602/https://blogs.lse.ac.uk/usappblog/2025/01/22/trump-rode-pain-and-victimhood-to-power-but-grievance-may-not-be-a-effective-basis-for-governing/ |archive-date=April 30, 2025 |access-date=May 1, 2025 |website=[[London School of Economics and Political Science]]}} The campaign team utilized [[Stanley George]] to lead outreach efforts targeting the Indian-American community, particularly focusing on immigration policies favoring Indian professionals and students.{{cite news |last=ചെറിയാൻ |first=പി പി |title=യുഎസ് റിപബ്ലിക്കൻ അഡ്വൻസറി ബോർഡിലേക്ക് സ്റ്റാൻലി ജോർജ് തിരഞ്ഞെടുക്കപ്പെട്ടു |url=https://globalindiannews.in/america/2021/07/12/stanley-george-appointed-to-us-republican-advisory-board/ |work=Global Indian News |date=2021-07-12 |access-date=2025-06-06 |language=ml }}{{Dead link|date=September 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }}{{cite web |title=Stanley George elected to US Republican advisory board |url=https://globalindiannews.in/america/stanly-george/ |website=Global Indian News |access-date=6 June 2025}}{{cite web |title=Malayali in Trump's campaign team |url=https://www.mathrubhumi.com/news/kerala/malayali-in-trumps-campaign-team-1.10076704 |website=Mathrubhumi |date=November 14, 2024 |access-date=6 June 2025}} ==== Marketing ==== Writing for ''Tilted Chair'', Kara Villarreal asserts that "Trump didn’t just run a political campaign; he launched a full-scale marketing movement".{{Cite web |last=Villarreal |first=Kara |date=November 12, 2024 |title=Trump's Masterclass in Marketing: How Trump Leveraged Proven Marketing Principles to Win the 2024 Election |url=https://tiltedchair.co/trumps-masterclass-in-marketing-how-trump-leveraged-proven-marketing-principles-to-win-the-2024-election/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250427202004/https://tiltedchair.co/trumps-masterclass-in-marketing-how-trump-leveraged-proven-marketing-principles-to-win-the-2024-election/ |archive-date=April 27, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |website=Tilted Chair}} The former President's messaging was simple, straightforward, and emotional, which analysts found engaged well with his "consumers"—voters. He appealed to their discontent over the economy, immigration, and national pride. One of the means he used to achieve this was mantras he would repeat during rallies, such as, "Are you better off now or four years ago?”, and, "I will fix it." Trump also relied on [[identity politics]] by creating an "[[In-group and out-group|us versus them]]" narrative. This, according to analysts, united his supporters and kept them motivated.{{Cite news |last=Dooley |first=Roger |date=November 6, 2024 |title=What CMOs And Marketers Can Learn From Trump's Successful Campaign |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/rogerdooley/2024/11/06/what-cmos-and-marketers-can-learn-from-trumps-successful-campaign/ |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250427202506/https://www.forbes.com/sites/rogerdooley/2024/11/06/what-cmos-and-marketers-can-learn-from-trumps-successful-campaign/ |archive-date=April 27, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |work=[[Forbes]]}} On advertising, Trump's campaign faced a massive financial disadvantage.{{Cite magazine |last=Kelly |first=Makena |date=November 6, 2024 |title=Donald Trump's Win Cements a New Era for Campaigning Online |url=https://www.wired.com/story/donald-trump-online-campaign-era/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250226183545/https://www.wired.com/story/donald-trump-online-campaign-era/ |archive-date=February 26, 2025 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |magazine=[[Wired (magazine)|Wired]]}}{{Cite web |last=Brousell |first=Heather |date=November 11, 2024 |title=Unpacking the Data Behind Trump and Harris' Presidential Election Ad Spending |url=https://www.ppchero.com/unpacking-the-data-behind-trump-and-harris-presidential-election-ad-spending/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241209042840/http://www.ppchero.com/unpacking-the-data-behind-trump-and-harris-presidential-election-ad-spending/ |archive-date=December 9, 2024 |access-date=April 27, 2025 |website=PPC Hero}} He, like Harris, concentrated ad funding on the seven [[swing state]]s. However, unlike his opponent, his funding was more localized, focusing more on individual voters than geographical groups. Trump's campaign spent more on [[YouTube]], [[Twitch (service)|Twitch]], [[Twitter]], and [[Streaming media|streaming services]], while Harris, on [[Google Chrome|Google]], [[Facebook]], [[Instagram]], and [[Snapchat]]. According to analysts, these two tactics made Trump's advertising strategy more adapted to modern trends, efficient, and ultimately, effective. They also enabled him to overcome his funding limitations.{{Cite news |last=Manning |first=Jamie |date=January 2, 2025 |title=2024 Was the 'Device ID' Election |url=https://www.wsj.com/opinion/the-device-id-election-2024-presidential-election-targeted-campaign-ads-7391758d |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250207003419/https://www.wsj.com/opinion/the-device-id-election-2024-presidential-election-targeted-campaign-ads-7391758d |archive-date=February 7, 2025 |access-date=April 28, 2025 |work=[[Wall Street Journal]]}} Trump targeted specific swing voters, or "streaming persuadables," while his opponent simply spent on the states at large. He did so by running highly customized ads exclusively in their households. As Trump [[Super PAC]] operative David Lee explained, "In the seven states, we were talking to 6.3 million people—they [Harris' campaign] were talking to 44.7 million"; thus, Harris was "wasting 85 percent of [her] money". In addition, the campaign's focus on streaming platforms over television networks catered to undecided voters, half of whom used only streaming and [[podcast]]s, not [[Cable television in the United States|cable]]. ==== Media ==== Trump's media strategy heavily relied on podcasts and online streaming. It largely, but not entirely, cast traditional forms aside, such as interviews on mainstream media outlets and even a ''[[60 Minutes]]'' appearance. Rather, the former President would interact with podcasters and [[YouTube]] content creators: [[Theo Von]], [[Patrick Bet-David]], [[Logan Paul]], et cetera,{{Cite news |last=Bade |first=Rachael |date=October 26, 2024 |title='All Trump, All the Time': Inside the Trump Campaign's Media Strategy |url=https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2024/10/26/trump-podcast-campaign-2024-elections-00185619 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250409163941/https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2024/10/26/trump-podcast-campaign-2024-elections-00185619 |archive-date=April 9, 2025 |access-date=April 21, 2025 |work=[[Politico]]}}{{Cite news |last1=Grynbaum |first1=Michael M. |last2=Koblin |first2=John |date=November 7, 2024 |title=A Master of the Media Evolved Yet Again in 2024 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2024/11/07/business/media/trump-media-strategy-podcasts.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250403173033/https://www.nytimes.com/2024/11/07/business/media/trump-media-strategy-podcasts.html |archive-date=April 3, 2025 |access-date=April 21, 2025 |work=[[The New York Times]]}}{{Cite news |last=Greenwood |first=Max |date=February 14, 2025 |title=How Trump Beat Out Harris Among News Influencers in 2024 |url=https://campaignsandelections.com/industry-news/influencers-favored-trump-in-2024/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250217090053/https://campaignsandelections.com/industry-news/influencers-favored-trump-in-2024/ |archive-date=February 17, 2025 |access-date=April 21, 2025 |work=[[Campaigns & Elections]]}} many of whom belonged to the [[manosphere]]. He would focus on apolitical matters: sports, family, [[extraterrestrial life]], more than politics. This strategy suited changing media trends, as more and more Americans were resorting to alternative sources for news over mainstream media, as well as being adapted to Trump's "circuitous and colloquial way of speaking". Young people—especially men—were particularly dependent on social media and podcasts for political coverage. On the other hand, Harris concentrated on traditional outlets. The former President garnered further media attention by visiting nonpolitical venues, such as [[American football|football]] games and [[McDonald's]]. By establishing a considerable presence on social media, Trump could home in on his tactic of dominating the news. His message was thus spread among more voters. According to [[Campaigns and Elections|''Campaigns & Elections'']], right-wing influencers posted about 2.5 times as much as left-wing influencers throughout the election. Trump's media strategy also bolstered [[Public image of Donald Trump|his image]]. By appearing on podcasts and YouTube videos, which are informal, homely, and unrestrained by design,{{Cite news |last=Modell |first=Maxwell |date=November 19, 2024 |title=US election shows how podcasts are shaping politics – and what the risks are |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cjr4dnv91p7o |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126173112/https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cjr4dnv91p7o |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |access-date=April 15, 2025 |work=[[The Conversation (website)|The Conversation]]}} he came across as approachable. They "humanize[d]" him. Jason Miller, remarked that Trump's media strategy, above all else, relied on "unscripted moments," which earned him more coverage and familiarity. Another benefit of non-traditional media outlets was that Trump could avoid fact-checks. For instance, in his interview with [[Joe Rogan]], he promoted [[Election denial movement in the United States|falsehoods about the 2020 election being stolen]] and exaggerations of his poll numbers. Michael M. Grynbaum and John Koblin of ''[[The New York Times]]'' noted that the "influencers he met with rarely challenged [him], and often lavished him with praise". Many of the most popular podcasts, including those that Trump had appeared on, would increasingly post political content with [[Conservatism in the United States|conservative]] messages in the lead up to the election.{{Cite news |last1=Alba |first1=Davey |last2=Yin |first2=Leon |last3=Love |first3=Julia |last4=Carman |first4=Ashley |last5=Bengani |first5=Priyanjana |last6=Dottle |first6=Rachael |last7=Mejía |first7=Elena |date=January 22, 2025 |title=How Popular YouTubers Pushed Young Male Voters Toward Trump |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/graphics/2025-youtube-podcast-men-for-trump/ |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250321031544/https://www.bloomberg.com/graphics/2025-youtube-podcast-men-for-trump/ |archive-date=March 21, 2025 |access-date=April 21, 2025 |work=[[Bloomberg News]]}} ==== Artificial intelligence ==== {{See also|Artificial intelligence and elections}} [[File:Protect our ducks and kittens in Ohio!.jpg|thumb|244x244px|A Trump-aligned committee sharing a [[deepfake]] of the candidate embracing a cat and duck, adding credibility to his [[Springfield pet-eating hoax]]]] Trump and his allies
